title: The Solar System
subtitle: Structure and scale of our cosmic neighbourhood

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: What keeps the planets in orbit around the Sun?
options: The pressure of solar wind pushing the planets
Gravitational attraction between the planets and the Sun
The planets are fixed in place on a crystalline sphere
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Planets orbit the Sun because of gravitational attraction. The Sun’s gravity constantly pulls the planet inward, providing the centripetal force needed to keep it in a roughly circular orbit.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Gravity from the Sun provides the centripetal force that keeps planets in orbit. The further a planet is, the weaker the gravitational pull and the slower its orbital speed.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.8.1 — Solar system

steps.title: The Solar System
steps.bodyHTML: The Solar System consists of the Sun (a star), eight planets and their moons, asteroids (rocky objects mainly between Mars and Jupiter), and comets (icy bodies with highly elliptical orbits). All objects orbit the Sun due to gravity, with planets following roughly circular orbits. The scale of the Solar System is enormous — the distance from Earth to the Sun is approximately 150 million km (1 AU), and the Solar System extends to roughly 100 AU.
commonErrors: Thinking the Sun orbits the Earth
Underestimating the vast scale of the Solar System

aqa.questionText: Describe the orbit of a comet around the Sun and explain how its speed changes during this orbit.

steps.label: Describe the shape of the orbit
steps.content: A comet follows a highly elliptical orbit around the Sun, unlike the roughly circular orbits of planets.

steps.label: Describe speed when close to the Sun
steps.content: When the comet is closest to the Sun, the gravitational pull is strongest, so it moves at its fastest speed.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — fastest when closest to Sun

steps.label: Describe speed when far from the Sun
steps.content: When the comet is furthest from the Sun, the gravitational pull is weakest, so it moves most slowly.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — slowest when furthest from Sun

aqa.finalAnswer: Elliptical orbit; fastest when closest to the Sun, slowest when furthest away.

aqa.questionText: Which of the following correctly describes an asteroid?

options.label: A
options.text: A large ball of ice and dust on a highly elliptical orbit around the Sun

options.label: B
options.text: A rocky object, most of which orbit the Sun between Mars and Jupiter

options.label: C
options.text: A moon that has escaped its planet’s gravity and now orbits the Sun

options.label: D
options.text: A small planet with its own moons and a circular orbit

markSchemeSteps: Asteroids are rocky objects; most occupy the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Option A describes a comet (icy, elliptical orbit), not an asteroid.
